data.title: Power Up Your Portfolio With Utility Dividends; Up To 8.3%
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

The internet has evolved into a vast, tangled web of information – much of it irrelevant, outdated, or simply unreliable. As the noise grows louder, it becomes harder to separate the signal from distraction. Most of us, whether individuals or corporations consume the internet through the products and services provided by technology companies. The same tech companies now believe it is essential that AI steps in to help us consume relevant content from the internet, indicating that this is more than just a convenience. Is AI error-prone and unreliable for some professions – absolutely. However, the same skepticism once surrounded cloud computing, and today, it forms a foundational pillar of modern business.

As with any transformative technology, trust builds through experience, adoption, and evolution. There is a high degree of speculation when you are trying to pick winners. Sure, Nvidia (NVDA) is leading the chip race, but do we expect chips to be at this level of shortage and cost forever? Moore’s law disagrees. Competition is bound to catch up, putting pressure on margins and valuations. The same goes for service providers. Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) is already facing concerns surrounding its Search segment and its potential for ad revenue. Apple (AAPL) is being blamed for its failure to deliver a competitive AI product. This churn is typical in any tech cycle – leaders stumble, laggards surge and massive capital is deployed to shift the balance. Some companies regain leadership, others fade into irrelevance (Nokia, Kodak, Yahoo, Blackberry, to name a few).

AI-driven search ad spending is set to surge to $26 billion by 2029, up from just $1 billion last year. Moreover, AI adoption is growing rapidly, with ~80% of U.S. banks notably increasing their spending towards AI. Yes, AI is extremely popular and is expected to continue growing in popularity over time. However, the majority of AI companies are currently not profitable. Even major tech firms are experiencing significant expenses related to AI infrastructure and services, often outpacing their AI-generated revenues. OpenAI reportedly spends over $700,000 a day to maintain its infrastructure. Similarly, Microsoft’s GitHub Copilot, priced at $10 per user per month, actually costs the company over $30 per user, presenting a significant drag on the bottom line.

Someone out there is making money hand over fist from the AI revolution, through large deals and visible multi-year contracts. Recently, Meta (META) signed a deal with Constellation Energy (CEG) to keep a nuclear reactor operational for 20 years to power its data centers. This presents a regular stream of revenues for the utility industry from highly credit-worthy customers.

Let us now examine how you can generate regular income from one of the foundational requirements for any technology – electricity.

Pick #1: UTF – Yield 7.2%

Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF) is diversified across 274 holdings, with 34% of its investments being electric utility, 11% midstream C-corp, and 8% gas distribution companies. Source

The CEF’s top holdings represent some of the largest electric, midstream, and gas distribution companies in the world, composing ~34% of the invested assets. UTF operates with a 29% leverage, with 81% of its borrowings carrying fixed rates, with a weighted average duration of 2.4 years. Every single company in the above list has significant deals with technology companies around data centers.

The active management and prudent use of leverage have resulted in UTF notably outperforming the passive utility index fund (XLU).

The benefit from UTF is steady monthly distributions of $0.1550/share, reflecting a 7.1% annualized yield. The CEF has maintained steady distributions to shareholders since its inception in 2004, having been through the Great Financial Crisis, the COVID-19 pandemic, and multiple cycles of rate hikes and cuts. Its distributions have been primarily a tax-friendly mix of QDI and LTCG. Over the past ten years, UTF has distributed approximately $19.684/share while preserving its NAV – highlighting the strength and resilience of its management’s strategy and execution.

Pick #2: SCE Preferreds – Up To 8.3% Yields

Southern California Edison (to be referred to as SCE in this article) is one of the largest electric utilities in the United States and is a subsidiary of Edison International (EIX). SCE serves ~15 million people in a 50,000 square-mile area of Central, Coastal, and Southern California and has been operating in the region for almost 140 years. Source

SCE has been in the headlines since the devastating fires in Los Angeles (January 2025), with allegations that its transmission towers sparked the Eaton wildfire in Altadena. There is still no determination of fault in these incidents, and ongoing litigation is pending in this matter. The company has submitted a three-year, $6.2 billion wildfire mitigation plan to regulators. The company expects this investment to be deployed between 2026 and 2028, including the installation of 440 circuit miles of covered conductors and 260 circuit miles of underground distribution lines.

Interestingly, SCE also seeks to raise customer rates by 10% to cover the costs of wildfire mitigation and certain operational expenses, including facilities and infrastructure. The California Public Utilities Commission is expected to make a decision this summer, and if approved, the rate hike would result in an average monthly increase of $18 in electrical bills for 15 million customers.

It is also notable that SCE has agreed to pay $82.5 million (as part of a settlement) to cover costs and damages from the 2020 Bobcat fire, representing a record settlement for the significant blaze in LA County. Notably, the settlement doesn’t admit wrongdoing or fault. If history is any guide, SCE (like any other major utility provider) will successfully pass on these costs to customers over time. California is the most wildfire-prone region in the U.S., and it is not surprising that the state has one of the highest electricity costs in the country.

Since the fires, EIX common stock has plunged to 12-year lows.

Nevertheless, the company recently reaffirmed its full-year core EPS guidance of $5.94–$6.34, placing its annualized dividend at a modest 64% payout ratio. Additionally, EIX management expressed continued confidence in delivering 5–7% Core EPS growth through 2028, placing the number between $6.74–$7.14, indicating solid room for continued dividend raises. We note that EIX maintains a 20-year track record of annual dividend raises to shareholders, and this isn’t changing anytime soon.

During the first three months of FY 2025, EIX reported a net income of $1.4 billion, while maintaining a cash position of $1.3 billion. We note that the company’s preferred dividend cost ~$44 million during the quarter, indicating strong income coverage for the preferred securities.

SCE has six outstanding cumulative preferreds, offering high yields at significantly discounted prices. We note that SCE preferreds pay qualified dividends.

  • SCE Trust II, 5.10% Cumulative Trust Preference (SCE.PR.G) – Yield 7.8%

  • SCE Trust IV, 5.375% Cumulative Fixed-to-Floating Rate Trust Preference (SCE.PR.J) – Yield 6%

  • SCE Trust V, 5.45% Cumulative Fixed-to-Floating Rate Trust Preference (SCE.PR.K) – Yield 6.1%

  • SCE Trust VI, 5.00% Cumulative Trust Preference (SCE.PR.L) – Yield 7.8%

  • SCE Trust VII, 7.50% Cumulative Trust Preference Securities (SCE.PR.M) – Yield 8.3%

  • SCE Trust VIII, 6.95% Cumulative Trust Preference Securities (SCE.PR.N) – Yield 8%.

At the time of this writing, the fixed rate SCE-N offers an 8% current yield and ~17% upside to par. With a May 2029 call date and a higher 6.95% coupon, we expect this to be a priority for redemption, depending on prevailing rates at the time. SCE-L presents a 7.8% yield and ~55% upside to par. With the lowest 5% coupon in the series, we expect this to be the last in line to be considered for redemption, allowing investors to collect these large QDI yields for longer.

Conclusion

Throughout its years of existence, the financial markets have operated amid uncertainty surrounding numerous factors, and this trend is likely to persist in the years ahead. Yet, there are certain things we will continue to consume in the years ahead.

Utilities are one of those sectors that will continue to see strong demand despite economic headwinds. They are well-positioned to maintain their profitability thanks to the non-discretionary nature of this service in the modern world. While many of today’s high-flying AI companies may fade into obscurity over time, the true winners, whatever they may be, will still be massive consumers of energy. And they’ll be paying millions to one or more of the utility providers held within UTF’s portfolio. With SCE preferreds, you gain priority access to the income stream of California’s second-largest public utility, in the most populous and economically powerful state in the U.S., with a GDP that surpasses many countries.

While the future is uncertain, one thing is clear: our reliance on technology, and by extension, our dependence on electricity, will only grow. Wall Street continues to stay euphoric about companies pouring money into AI. At High Dividend Opportunities, we prefer investments in sectors that generate cash, not spend it. The lights stay on – and the dividends keep flowing. This is the beauty of our Income Method.

data.title: Energy Transfer Has Become An Energy Empire To Buy Hand Over Fist
data.description:
Introduction

Energy Transfer (NYSE:ET) is a major midstream company that operates one of the largest and most diversified portfolios of energy assets, primarily focused on the transportation and processing of natural gas and crude oil.

I have to admit that ET holds a special place in my heart as it was one of my first purchases back in early 2021 when it was trading in the high $5s levels (how I wish that I bought more back then) following an article by Bloomberg with the 50 companies to watch for the year. The company now represents 16.5% of my overall portfolio.

I believe that Energy Transfer can still provide substantial returns despite the units appreciating greatly since my purchase and that the company is prone to keep on growing with its newfound capital discipline.

Operation Overview

ET has so many different revenue streams that it is hard to count as the company has become a true energy empire that covers all possible midstream operations.

Intrastate T&S consists of intrastate pipelines that are locked in states such as Texas with key assets like the 6,900 miles Houston Pipeline system and the Oasis Pipeline. It also includes around 50 Bcf of working gas storage capacity and, in all, has a throughput of 12 Bcf/d which also interconnects with interstate pipelines for broader access. The segment generated $3.43B in revenue for the past 12 months.

Interstate T&S operates long-haul nat gas pipelines connecting major production basins to downstream markets. Key assets include the Transwestern Pipeline, a 2,600-mile pipeline connecting the Permian and Rockies to California, and the 6,000-mile Panhandle Eastern Pipeline serving Midwest markets. Total throughput reaches around 15 Bcf/d and the segment has a storage capacity of 70 Bcf. The sector generates less sales than its intrastate counterpart, but it still comes at an honorable $2.3B for the LTM.

Midstream englobes all nat gas gathering, processing, and treating within major U.S. basins such as the Permian, Eagle Ford, and Marcellus. The segment has 12 Bcf/d of nat gas processing capacity and around 40,000 miles of total pipelines. The segment generated $12B for the past 12 months.

NGL and Refined Products T&S treats the logistics behind the transportation of NGLs and products like Gasoline. Assets include the Mariner East System and the Lone Star Express Pipeline. It totals 1MM bpd of fractionation capacity, mainly in Mont Belvieu, and has around 1.5 million bpd of NGL throughput. The sector accounted for $27.1B in sales for the LTM.

ET also has strictly crude T&S segments that include pipelines such as the Dakota Access that can transport 570,000 bpd and the Bayou Bridge that moves crude from Texas to Louisiana refineries. Total storage reaches 50MM bpd, including at Cushing, and throughput at 5 million bpd. This is the largest segment at the moment sales-wise, at $27.1B for the LTM.

Outside of strict ET operations, the energy company also has key investments, mainly in Sunoco but also in USA Compression Partners. Sunoco has around 10,000 miles of refined products pipelines and thousands of gas stations and convenience stores across the U.S. It provides a downstream arm for ET that offers synergies for its midstream operations. The USAC stake, though smaller, offers a fleet of compression units that supports gas pipelines with around 3.7MM horsepower in capacity and processing plants.

Other smaller operations include the Lake Charles LNG project that is pending regulatory approval in Louisiana and will be able to export around 16 MTPA once completed. It also includes RNG facilities and CCS pilot projects.

Current Dynamics

ET has seen overall throughput volumes grow, which boosted the company’s Q1 financials. That reflects higher demand across its transportation services, and NGLs have remained a key area for growth for the company. Despite this, unit prices have dropped from their recent highs of over $21 due to the current backdrop of volatile energy prices, geopolitical tensions, and debt concerns.

Despite this, ET has doubled its investment plan of $5B in growth projects for the year as overall demand for NGLs is supporting the company’s plans to expand its NGL terminals. Plus, the company’s assets are well-placed to supply energy to large data centers in the country, which have become a real tailwind for midstream companies. ET already supplies 185 power plants and could significantly increase that number over the next few years. I believe that these in-house developments could favor unitholders in the near future as ET has a long history of aggressive M&A that has been proven to be costly.

I’ll say that ET’s empire-building endeavor has given the company a great competitive advantage but has come at a cost as more and more of its earnings are tied to the commodity energy market, which has proved to be highly volatile since the pandemic. Although it generally turned in the company’s favor as the share of its gas transportation is shrinking with over half of ET’s cash flow coming from crude, NGLs, and crude marketing that tend to be more sensitive. However, I believe that ET wants to retain exposure to more volatile markets to capture wider spreads thanks to their immense knowledge in the sector.

Valuation

ET currently has a poor P/E Non-GAAP when compared to the broad sector at 15.15, around 28% higher than the sector median, while also boasting a pricey EV/EBITDA of 9.37, close to 40% higher than the sector median. What impresses me has been ET’s capability to shift from a -$6B FCF deficit to a massive +$6B FCF with its transition from a period of heavy investments to debt reduction and DCF stability. The company currently has an FCF yield of 9.9% as the stock keeps on rising. CapEx has risen, yet I expect the company to keep a strong FCF position.

The company’s FCF growth is even more impressive when comparing it to peers such as Kinder Morgan or Enterprise, which have suffered from lower energy prices since 2022. I believe that the growth trend for the company will continue with its strategic projects in the Permian and future Data center deals. The LNG ambition is also welcomed as it is a natural extension of ET’s business model. Although, I worry about the growing number of LNG terminals competing for the same client pool. Overall throughput increased drastically over the past 3 years, with around 90% fee-based revenue, which translates into an impressive EBITDA growth of 10.6% during that time.

ET expects Adj. EBITDA to come between $16.1 and $16.5B for FY 2025, which would translate into a strong 14.95% growth rate at the top of the range. That's truly impressive as it would outpace the growth rate of the past 3 years, and that is thanks to strategic deals such as its long-term agreement with Cloudburst Data Centers and other LNG deals. Following the ramp-up in CapEx, I expect EBITDA growth to accelerate through 2030, therefore increasing distribution to unitholders.

Current margins can be seen as low, with its current gross margin at 19.43%, about half of the sector median. EBITDA margin is also at around the same mark at 17.77%. However, ET has proved to be tremendously effective at capturing profits during volatile times and even weather complications such as the 2021 winter storm in Texas. The business model of ET allows the firm to raise reservation fees even when throughput drops, which can keep margins steady through down cycles.

One of the goals of the company post-pandemic was to keep its leverage ratio within a 4–4.5 range, which has been easily achieved by management. Since 2015, Net Debt to EBITDA has dropped from 7.9 to 4.1 while despite seeing higher interest rates, ET’s EBITDA/Interest Expense increased from 2.8 to 4.5.

Looking at overall distribution, ET spooked many income-focused investors in 2020 when it cut in half its overall distribution. In my mind, this was the right move in an uncertain economic background, which allowed me to snatch shares at such a low price. Since, ET has exceeded its goals by increasing quarterly distribution for the 14th consecutive quarter, and I expect similar growth despite ramped-up CapEx. The current yield has come back to 7.24% thanks to the recent drop in share prices, which, I believe, is a good entry point for investors, especially considering the 23.95% 3Y CAGR growth rate.

Risks

At the end of the day, ET is dependent on energy volumes and the overall health of the O&G sector. While most of its revenue is fee-based, a slowdown in upstream production could reduce overall sales for the company. Further renewable energy pushes are also inverse to the company’s business model, which, if the trend accelerates, could significantly reduce the company’s revenue and cause huge asset writedowns.

ET’s asset base consists of aging pipelines that always require maintenance to avoid leaks and outages that could drastically reduce margins or face fines or even lawsuits. This could reduce throughput and therefore strain ET’s ability to service its debt and distribution.

Conclusion

Energy Transfer is such a great company in my view, with a bright future ahead from strategic projects like Lake Charles LNG and the incoming demand from data centers. The current distribution yield offers a good entry for investors, and I expect management to keep its leverage ratio in line. I give Energy Transfer a strong buy.

data.title: Progress Software: A Proven Acquirer At A Reasonable Price
data.description:
In the world of real estate investing, there are a host of different personas. Among the host of characters are the luxe developers and the stable renovators.

The luxe developers get all of the glitz and glam. They are visionary and have grand plans for what buildings, streets, and neighborhoods will look like in the future. They're known for having a high inclination for risk and are often willing to risk it all on legacy-defining projects.

When things pan out correctly, the luxe developers look like geniuses. Their flashy new office buildings, condos, or multi-use developments gain lots of attention. However, things don't always pan out. From time to time, things go south, and the developer is left scrambling trying to pull together the cash to cover their construction loan.

This differs from that of the stable renovator. This persona only acquires large, stable, cash flowing properties. And more often than not, they love it when the property is just a bit out of date. When this is the case, they can go in and lightly renovate the property, fix it up, and drastically improve the value of the asset. They're not in it for the glitz and glam. Instead, they're looking for stable returns backed by healthy, predictable assets.

While the market has a host of companies akin to the luxe developers, there are also some great companies that align with the persona of the stable renovator. Progress Software Corporation (NASDAQ:PRGS) is one such company.

At one point or another, virtually every company comes to a crossroads in their growth story. They've found that their top-line growth has been leveling off and are left with a decision to make. Are there other ways that they can invest in their product to reignite revenue growth? Or are there other similar companies that we can acquire and fold into our operations? While the latter option is often a very different road to embark on, it can pay sizable dividends if done right.

Progress is one of the companies that has pulled off this transition. With time, they have turned into serial acquirers. Now, many different brands have been folded into the Progress umbrella, ultimately creating the Progress that we know today.

Levered Capital Structure

Many investors are quick to call out Progress' levered capital structure. They have a market cap of $2.7 billion, but an enterprise value of $4.1 billion. This delta is created by Progress' $1.5 billion of total debt, which far outweighs its $124.2 million of cash on their balance sheet.

Many people view this as an overly burdensome load of debt. After all, most software companies that are listed on public exchanges have little to no debt to speak of. But those are the same companies that will end as a boom or bust. Either their expensive growth pays off, or they'll no longer be relevant. In a way, those companies are like the luxe developers.

But a company like Progress is looking for stable, cash flowing assets. And when those assets become available, they are more than willing to raise capital to fund the acquisitions.

In the most recent quarter, Progress paid $18.4 million in net interest expenses. If this number were to be annualized, they would be paying close to $80 million of interest every year.

While this is a large sum to be paying to lenders, it's important to point out that Progress generated almost $300 million of levered free cash flow over the last four quarters.

At the end of the day, Progress is taking a page out of the playbooks of private equity firms. They acquire stable, cash flowing assets. They then lever them up with as much debt as they can reasonably afford. This financing decision not only lets them acquire more cash flow with a smaller equity injection, but it also creates the environment for what is often referred to as agency theory.

Oftentimes, mature companies with strong cash flows run into a conflict of interest. The investors want the company to return as much cash to shareholders as possible. On the other hand, healthy cash flows can entice a company's management to indulge in things such as pet projects, lavish expenses, etc. But by levering the company up, the severity of this predicament is tempered. Companies with larger loads of debt are subject to higher interest payments and financing obligations. This reality forces management to be disciplined with their resources.

While some may knock Progress for its debt levels, particularly relative to other software companies, I don't think it's an apples-to-apples comparison. In a way, Progress is playing an entirely different game than these other companies. They're in the acquisitions business, while others are still chasing organic growth. As such, I don't think it's fair to knock Progress for their discrete capital structure.

Valuation

Seeking Alpha's quant system rates Progress' overall valuation as a 'C-.' Generally speaking, it's rated favorably in the metrics that start with EBITDA and move lower down the income statement, such as EBIT, earnings, and cash flow.

Currently, Progress trades for an EV/Revenue multiple of 5.1x, an EV/EBITDA multiple of 15x, and a Price/Cash Flow multiple of 12.9x. It's worth noting that Progress' year-over-year growth rates for revenue, EBITDA, and cash from operations are 12.9%, 16.6%, and 6.2%.

One company worth comparing with Progress is OpenText Corporation (OTEX). Much like Progress, OpenText is a serial acquirer of B2B software assets with recurring, stable revenues.

From the outside, OpenText can make Progress look relatively expensive. Currently, OpenText is trading for an EV/Revenue multiple of 2.4x, an EV/EBITDA multiple of 8.6x, and a Price/Cash Flow multiple of 8.5x. Each of these multiples are several turns lower than what Progress trades for. However, a closer look under the hood shows that Progress' economic engine is operating at a far superior clip than OpenText's.

Year-over-year revenue growth for OpenText is -11.5%. For a company that relies on debt financing to fund their acquisitions, much like Progress, shrinking revenues are a major concern. For this reason, I believe that OpenText's suppressed multiples relative to Progress are merited.

Another company worth comparing with Progress is Constellation Software (OTCPK:CNSWF). For those that are unfamiliar, Constellation Software is essentially the holy grail of acquisitive software holding companies. They've grown their strategy to impressive heights, as they now eclipse over $10 billion of annual revenue.

In many ways, I believe that Constellation represents the valuation ceiling for Progress. And as things stand, Constellation trades for an EV/Revenue multiple of 7.6x, an EV/EBITDA multiple of 41.3x, and a Price/Cash Flow multiple of 33.3x. But, it's important to note that these multiples are likely suppressed because Constellation trades on a Canadian exchange. If they were to instead be listed on a US exchange such as the NYSE or NASDAQ, they would likely command even higher multiples.

With this in mind, I don't think it would be unrealistic for Progress to trade for a multiple similar to that of Constellation's Canadian-exchange-suppressed multiple. Regardless, I believe that Progress currently trades at a fair price for long-term investors.

What to Watch for in Q2 Earnings

With Progress' Q2 earnings just around the corner, there are a couple of key developments that investors should monitor. First, the progress and performance of the ShareFile integration will be noteworthy. Progress' executives shared in their Q1 earnings call that their tuck-in of ShareFile was "on track or ahead of plan," and that the full integration should be complete by the close of fiscal year 2025.

The success of the ShareFile acquisition will play a key role in shaping Progress' future trajectory. One metric that investors can track to gauge the advancement of the integration is the business unit's operating margin. Management is targeting a 40% margin for the unit, largely fueled by synergies from the consolidation with Progress.

A second development to follow in the Q2 earnings release is Progress' ARR trajectory. From Q4 2024 to Q1 2025, Progress saw a marginal dip in their ARR. Anthony Folger, Progress' CFO, provided color on the occurrence:

"Sometimes Q4 to Q1, we will see a slight, I'll call it, a seasonal dip in ARR. And then we generally see that bounce back in the second quarter. If you were to look back at some of our Q1 earnings calls, maybe '23 or '22, you'd probably see a similar trend line. So nothing out of the ordinary from our perspective."

While this is reassuring commentary from Folger, the numbers never lie. Investors should closely watch the ARR number to see if it accelerates as it has in previous quarters and years.

Risks

It's no secret that Progress is in the notoriously difficult M&A business. Progress' future rides on their continued ability to find, fund, and integrate acquisitions. Any of those steps along the way can introduce bumps or roadblocks that could potentially derail their growth strategy. And with the large recent acquisition of ShareFile, the integration risk looms large.

Progress' management issued sizable sums of debt to fund the acquisition. And of course, they believe it will be a net-positive outcome, or else they wouldn't have wasted their resources on it. Regardless, their ability to extract the synergies that they planned for will be key for the integration. Should they fall short on their targets, Progress stock could take a hit. But if the past is a worthwhile indicator, Progress is well-equipped.

Conclusion

Progress has built a compelling business. They have become serial acquirers of software business, and operate their company with the financial acumen that you'd expect from an M&A-reliant firm. They're more than willing to take big swings, such as the ShareFile acquisition, and have demonstrated their ability to turn those bets into accretive outcomes. Moreover, their valuation appears reasonable, if not slightly undervalued, relative to peers. In all, they paint a compelling case for a 'Buy' rating for long-term investors.

data.title: Wingstop: High Valuation But Favorable Long-Term Prospects
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Pod Investor as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Introduction

The restaurant industry has rebounded particularly well post-pandemic. Industry sales have continued growing past their 2019 peak. Consumers have also responded well, with consumer spending on food away from home peaking in 2024 at $1.539 trillion in the US. The industry has also kept on innovating, particularly in the fast-casual space, where restaurants aim to provide quality food that is freshly produced (vs typical fast food) but is quicker to deliver (vs a typical restaurant). Companies such as Sweetgreen (SG), Shake Shack (SHAK), CAVA Group (CAVA), and Chipotle (CMG) have made headlines in space, but from an investor standpoint, the one I find most attractive is Wingstop Inc. (NASDAQ:WING), a company that arguably does not receive much attention.

I recommend a HOLD rating as opposed to street-majority BUY due to Wingstop's strong growth outlook and catalysts that would allow Wingstop to differentiate themselves from competitors in the fast-casual space. However, its current stock price compared to its valuation implies that near-term growth surprises are priced in, making long-term developments in the next year important for the stock.

Business Model

The first Wingstop opened in 1994, continuing its focus of serving chicken wings (as well as tenders and sandwiches) today. Given its unique focus on one product, it aims to diversify its menu by offering 12 permanent flavors, created by proprietary Wingstop recipes. In addition, it offers limited time special flavors to attract new customers and keep current customers satisfied while not straying away from their brand.

From a business model standpoint, Wingstop's franchisor model is by far its key differentiator. 98% of its stores are owned and operated by independent franchisees, allowing Wingstop to be a capital-light model while having 2,689 restaurants worldwide. With a firm branding and balance sheet favorable business model, Wingstop is in the perfect position to deliver on its ambitious growth plans.

Growth Focus

The rapid growth phases that companies such as McDonald's (MCD) and Chipotle went through is similar to where Wingstop is today. Wingstop is primarily focused on growth in two areas: 1) sustaining a gradual increase in same-store sales growth and more importantly, 2) building more stores to expand its reach.

1.0 Restaurant Expansion

In Wingstop's latest annual filing, management said they wanted to be "a Top 10 Global Restaurant Brand," by growing their restaurant total to 10,000 stores (6000 domestic, 4000 international). As of late, the number of new store openings has increased quite rapidly over the last few years (graph below). As of Q1 2025 (not pictured), Wingstop has 2,689 restaurants, opening 126 units within a quarter, implying a near 500+ projected store openings by the end of the year. This would be the largest increase in its history and a nearly 40% YoY increase in the number of new restaurant openings. These numbers, in line with management's growth plans, reflect the growing economies of scale Wingstop is able to achieve with more stores (increased brand reputation, consumer data, etc.) that would further allow Wingstop to capitalize on its desired number of store openings to boost top-line figures.

2.0 Unit Economics and Same-Store Sales

Given that 98% of stores are owned by independent franchisees, Wingstop makes an effort to sustain attractive store unit economics to appeal to potential franchisees who are crucial in opening new locations. Wingstop's domestic average unit volume (AUV) grew to approximately $2.1 million by the end of 2024, growing steadily over the past few years, increasing approximately 11.6% per year on average since 2018. On top of that, its average initial investment for a new store opening is $535,000 - which is relatively low compared to other similar franchises (McDonald's, Domino's (DPZ), Wendy's (WEN), making it attractive among current franchisees, who made up 90% of restaurants opened in 2023 and 2024. Benefitting from the business expertise of existing franchisee operators, Wingstop can grow new stores effectively, targeting a "franchisee unlevered cash-on-cash return of 70%" in the second year of operations for newly opened restaurants. Its low entry investment and high returns make it favorable for franchisees, who are critical in its expansion plans.

Catalysts

1.0 Smart Kitchens

Competitors who are also going through critical growth stages in the fast-casual space (Shake Shack, Sweetgreen) have cited concerns regarding providing quick service while maintaining a reputation for creating quality, freshly made food. The system-wide implementation of "Smart Kitchens" solely in Wingstop units by the end of the year would allow Wingstop to capitalize on this major issue in the industry, positioning the company to expand on its market share.

Around mid-May, two weeks after Q1 earnings, Wingstop hosted analysts at its Dallas headquarters detailing its plan to reach $3M in AUV. The main focus of its plan was its adoption of AI-driven "Smart Kitchens." Smart Kitchens digitize tickets and orders, a shift away from old-school paper tickets Wingstop used, while also featuring consumer-facing order status screens to manage customer expectations regarding order times. The proprietary systems aim to reduce ticket times by 50%, from ~20 to 10 mins, and decrease order times overall, a huge differentiator in the fast-casual space where order times have become a major pain point for restaurants such as Shake Shack. As of May 16, 2025, the AI systems were in nearly 400 stores, with 50 units having implemented the system for a year now.

Historically, order times used to range from 30 to 40 minutes at Wingstop during busy times, detracting customers who filtered for fast ordering through apps or websites. However, Smart Kitchen Units during the 2025 Super Bowl averaged 16 minutes, a drastic improvement. These new improvements sought to improve operational efficiency while attracting new consumers who would have otherwise been detracted by longer wait times (> 20 mins), an issue for competitors. The implementation would aim to boost revenue by attracting customers by competing on order times, giving the stock price room to benefit as this development plays out in the next few quarters.

2.0 Chicken Vs. Beef

On the commodity side, beef prices have been rising much faster than chicken prices in the last few years (graph below), with chicken prices remaining relatively stable through inflationary periods. Given the new macroeconomic environment of the inflation-growth tradeoff, if chicken-wing prices remain inflation-resistant, Wingstop is well positioned to build a slowly accumulating competitive advantage.

By no surprise, chicken is the largest product cost item and represented 57.6% of Wingstop's purchases for the 2024 fiscal year. In addition, Wingstop currently offers no beef products on its menu, insusceptible to increasing beef price risk. In the face of these commodity pressures, Wingstop gains to stand a competitive advantage from other growing fast-casual competitors who offer beef products (Shake Shack, Portillo's (PTLO), Jack in the Box (JACK)), capitalizing on potential consumer preference shifts from beef to chicken, benefitting from its specialized expertise and size in the chicken wing industry.

3.0 Stock Repurchase Program

On December 5, 2024, Wingstop announced a $500 million stock repurchase program on top of a $250 million repurchase authorization from August 2023. As of its latest 10-Q filing, Wingstop still has approximately $191,312,121 in dollar value of shares outstanding that they can repurchase, providing a floor for the stock price which has been rising, but is yet to touch its late 2024 highs.

What sticks out to me is management's incentive. In times of Wingstop's strongest growth, management has made it clear they want to provide to shareholders in the face of strong future expectations. When they announced their share repurchase program back in December, CFO Alex Kaleida said, "We believe our asset-lite, highly franchised model enables industry-leading shareholder returns." This repurchase program was authorized in spite of a retained deficit (negative retained earnings), which was -$675,586,00 in December 2024 and -$714,982,000 as of March 2025, implying that management believes that Wingstop is approaching its rapid expansionary phase as detailed by their growth progress above.

Valuation

1.0 Comparable Companies Analysis

Although I believe that Smart Kitchens is a strong growth catalyst, the other two catalysts are long-term developments that I do not believe will play out in the next quarter (Q2) or even the one after that (Q3). For Wingstop to start gaining additional market share due to its progress in addressing order time issues would be developments that I would expect to start taking effect in Q4 or even 2026 Q1. Therefore, my current valuation, which underestimates the current stock price by a significant amount, is a sign that Wingstop's stock at the current moment may be pricing in growth and I do not believe buying at this level until accelerated growth is further seen throughout the quarters (consistent store expansion, sustained same-store sales).

In my valuation, I used traditional fast-casual competitors such as Shake Shack, Sweetgreen, Jack in the Box, and Portillo's but also included developed franchise brands such as McDonald's and Wendy's, who are comparable, but more established leaders in the space. Using an average and 75th percentile EV/EBITDA metric, I derived the following implied stock prices below.

2.0 Discounted Cash Flow Analysis

Below is a summary of the operating model and discounted cash flow analysis I constructed using FactSet aggregate estimates for 2025-2027 data and CapIQ aggregate estimates for 2028-2029 data. The model shows that a majority of future revenue growth comes from franchisee fees, consistent with restaurant growth aided by independent franchisees. The EBIT margin also steadily grows to 28.6% by the end of 2029, allowing for an increase in revenue growth to factor into an increase in unlevered free cash flow.

For 2028 and 2029, I used a trailing average for net working capital given how variable the change was from year to year, making a forecast of NWC as a % of revenue unreasonable. The main reason I used street estimates was to show that even with the street projected fundamentals, the implied share price using the forecasted EV/EBITDA multiple of 2029, 31.2, still comes in reasonably lower than the current share price and in line with the comparable analysis.

Although both metrics imply Wingstop is trading above its fair value, I think the valuation is a better indication of whether the stock is over-priced rather than what price it should be trading at. At this moment, Wingstop is in a unique position - growing faster than its competitors, but not big enough compared to traditional fast-food industry leaders. So, I think that it is reasonable that the stock trades above its fundamental value due to a growth premium. But given this fact, a further increase in stock price would need to be justified by fundamental growth that beats already optimistic street estimates. I think the stock price as of now is justified but may serve as a deterrent to buy given the longer-term nature of the developments that would need to play out for a sustained increase in stock price. Thus, I gave Wingstop a HOLD.

Risks

1.0 Franchisee Financing

According to their 2024 10-K, Wingstop does not provide financing to independent franchisees. Given how integral independent franchisees are opening new stores (made up 90% of new restaurants in '23 & '24), turbulent conditions in credit markets, specifically traditional or SBA loans, would directly hurt revenue growth.

2.0 Commodity Price Risk

Despite fixed arrangements with many of their suppliers, Wingstop does not have an "established fixed price markets for fresh bone-in chicken wings, so we may be subject to prevailing market conditions." (FY-2024 10-K). Bone-in chicken wings accounted for 19.8% cost of sales in 2024 and according to the 2024 10-K, a "hypothetical 10.0% increase in the bone-in chicken wing costs in fiscal year 2024 would have increased company-owned restaurant costs of sales by approximately $1.8 million during the year." This translates to a roughly 2% increase in cost of sales (Wingstop defines cost of sales as: all operating expenses of company-owned restaurants, including advertising expenses, but excludes depreciation and amortization), which would impact the lucrative operating margins that Wingstop reaps from its asset-light franchisor model.

Conclusion

Overall, I believe Wingstop has favorable long-term prospects as a business. Its growth plan remains strong, backed by its accelerating progress and appeal of individual units. Operational improvements and its standing in the chicken wing space give it the ability to capture market share from other fast-casual brands, who have had trouble resolving issues such as order times. However, its stock price is trading at near highs, implying near-term growth may be priced, but long-term developments are promising. Thus, I give Wingstop a HOLD for now and would look to revising this to either a BUY or retaining the rating based on growth progress seen in Q2 and Q3.

data.title: Eastman Chemical: Spending Cuts And China Détente Support Shares (Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Eastman Chemical Company (NYSE:EMN) have been a significant underperformer over the past year, losing a quarter of their value and sitting near 52-week lows. The entire chemicals sector has performed poorly, amid concerns about global growth, weak Chinese construction activity, and the potential negative ramifications of US tariffs. I last covered shares of Eastman in April, rating the stock a "sell." While EMN has yet to move into the mid-$60's, the stock has been a tremendous underperformer, gaining just 2% while the market has rallied back 20%, justifying the sell rating. Given how much the stock has lagged, it is worth seeing if shares may now offer a better relative opportunity.

Over the past three months, markets have been focused intensely on what the tariff policy of the United States will ultimately be, and as of now, there is still considerable uncertainty. While a return to uniform, retaliatory tariffs is highly unlikely in my view, we are nearing the end of the 90-day pause during which there is a 10% baseline tariff. So far, only the UK has secured a deal. Over the next two weeks, there will likely be a scramble for deals, some delays, and some incremental tariff increases, depending on the country.

Eastman is a relatively unique company in this context because it is a net exporter. As such, its business is more impacted by retaliatory tariffs than by US tariffs. On the Q1 earnings call, management cited $30 million of Q2 tariff costs. It did not provide H2 guidance, given the unpredictability of where tariffs will land. Over the past two months, I believe the company has received some incremental good news on tariffs.

As a net exporter, one of its biggest exposures is on sales to China. Indeed, 60% of China's sales are supplied from the U.S. During the peak of tariff tensions, there was a 100+% tariff on these imports, which essentially made it impossible to sell product to China, greatly impairing its business. As part of the current truce, there is just a 10% tariff from China on its US Imports. This is far more manageable, and given US cost advantages due to lower natural gas, a 10% tariff is not likely to materially reduce US chemical exports to China in my view. Now, there is considerable uncertainty as to how long this truce can last, or if each nation is buying time while trying to secure other supplies for key products. However, if tariffs remain at 10%, this would be better than I feared in early April.

That said, there are still second-round tariff impacts that impact EMN. Most notably, tariffs do risk reducing economic activity, which in turn will weaken chemical companies' earnings power. EMN has meaningful cyclical exposure. Construction is about 11% of revenue, with autos close to 20%. The auto sector appears likely to face a persistent 25% tariff, while businesses may delay cap-ex plans amid high rates and economic uncertainty. As you can see below, construction spending has been steadily slowing and is now in negative territory.

While not necessarily a "tariff cost," the extent to which the industrial economy is slowing is an ongoing headwind for the business that I expect to persist. It also appears unlikely that the Fed will be meaningfully reducing interest rates, which will continue to weigh on construction sector activity and EMN results by proxy.

Even as overall the tariff posture is better than feared, especially regarding China, there has other incremental negative news. As part of its effort to roll back Biden-era energy policies, the Trump Administration is revoking a $375 million grant for an EMN facility in Texas. On the last earnings call, EMN management was confident that funding would persist. This has proven not to be the case, questioning the future viability of the project. It is scheduled to be completed in 2028. Given the multiyear nature of the project, there is no near-term revenue impact, and I would expect the company to defer work on it or even cancel the project entirely if there is no legal recourse to restore funding.

Looking at recent results, in the company's first quarter reported on April 24th, Eastman earned $1.91, which did beat estimates by $0.02 even as revenue was down 1% to $2.3 billion. EMN generated $311 million of EBIT, up $6 million sequentially, with margins holding flat at 13.6%. Volumes fell by 1%.

I would emphasize that Q1 results do not have any tariff impact, which will begin to hit results this quarter. Indeed, it is guiding to $1.70-$1.90 in Q2 EPS, which was about $0.40 below consensus at the time. EMN results usually improve sequentially in Q2, given the seasonality of construction spending as weather improves, but that appears unlikely this year. Management is no longer providing full-year EPS guidance, given the magnitude of uncertainties.

Because of the weak environment, EMN is redoubling its effort to cut costs. It is halfway through a $50 million cost-cutting program. Given economic uncertainty, it is upsizing the plan to achieve $75 million of run-rate cost savings. Additionally, EMN cut its 2025 cap-ex plan to $550 million from $700-800 million. With funding pulled for its TX facility, further cap-ex cuts are possible.

Its advanced materials unit is an example of how cost-cutting is helping EMN manage through the down cycle. As you can see, revenue was down 4% from last year, with both volumes and pricing down. However, EBIT was up $9 million thanks to 120bps of margin expansion. A significant part of this was its Kingsport facility ramping up activity. Its new Kingsport facility is performing well, providing $75 million of EBITDA uplift this year from 2024. Still, weakness in the auto market and in China clouds the go-forward outlook.

Its additives business was the relative bright spot, with revenue up 4% from last year, thanks to better volumes and pricing. Margins expanded 80bps, leading to a 10% jump in EBIT to $141 million.

Destocking has hurt its fiber business, which was down 12% on a volume basis. Revenue declined by 13% to $288 million, leading to a $15 million drop in EBIT to $88 million. Chinese demand for textiles was particularly weak. This is an area where EMN may struggle indirectly from tariffs. With a 55% tariff from the US on China imports, Chinese apparel manufacturers are likely to face substantial headwinds, which will reduce their need to buy fibers from EMN. I view the apparel sector as the most exposed to tariffs.

Importantly, for this period of uncertainty, EMN has a solid balance sheet with 2.3x debt to EBITDA. It has fairly well-stagged maturities, though refinancing its 2026 maturity next year will likely lead to some modest incremental interest expense. Given its solid balance sheet, I view its 4.4% dividend as secure, and management targets a modest $100-200 million of buybacks.

Back in April, I was targeting $6.25 in go-forward earnings power and $450 million of free cash flow. I did expect calendar 2025 results to be a bit stronger than this, due to the fact tariffs only began to hit the business in Q2. This run-rate implied about $6.75 in 2025 EPS. With some incremental cost cuts and a less aggressive retaliatory stance from China, I view the macro backdrop a bit better.

As such, I now expect about $7.00 of calendar year EPS and ~$6.50 on a 12-month forward basis. This remains a bit below the $7.40 consensus, but with shares having underperformed so much, I believe markets are already pricing in some future downward revisions. I do remain very cautious on the ~10% growth analysts assume next year, as I do not see signs of a reacceleration in industrial activity.

Importantly, with a much lower cap-ex trajectory, this business should generate at least $650 million of free cash flow now, and I see scope for EMN to continue deferring expansion activities. This leaves the stock with a ~7.5% free cash flow yield. I have generally valued commodity chemical companies with solid balance sheets in the 7-8% zone.

As a result, unless we see a recession, which causes another leg lower in revenue, it appears the stock is now more adequately pricing in the current environment. Between some moderation on trade and aggressive cost-cut actions, EMN results may not be as bad as once feared, even as I do not see material recovery coming. Having already seen such significant relatively underperformance, I do not believe EMN merits a sell rating at this point.

As such, I am moving shares to a "hold" with the stock fairly valued. I see no rush to buy shares, given the slow recovery and modest buyback activity—I would wait for a dip in the $60's to buy, should such an opportunity to materialize. But, shares have sufficiently de-risked relative to the market that selling is no longer appropriate at this time.

data.title: CDX: Good Idea, Bad Execution
data.description:
Back in November, I recommended the Simplify High Yield ETF (CDX) as a good way to own high-yield bonds, as the fund uses an equity hedge to protect against downside risks. However, given my recent disappointment with the Simplify Enhanced Income ETF (HIGH), I wanted to revisit the CDX ETF, to make sure Simplify continues to manage the fund as per its mandate.

At first glance, the CDX ETF appears to be doing okay, delivering 5.6% in total returns since November, even better than the S&P 500 Index (Figure 1).

However, there are two notable periods in the fund's price history that are worth investigating (Figure 2).

The first sharp drop in CDX's price history occurred on December 23, 2024. This one is easy to explain, as the CDX paid a large $0.9471 / share distribution that went ex. dividend on the date (Figure 3).

The second period of volatility occurred in early April, and understanding this volatility requires us to revisit CDX's strategy.

Brief Fund Overview

For those not familiar with the CDX ETF, the Simplify High Yield PLUS Credit Hedge ETF is a high-yield bond fund that owns total return swaps that give the fund exposure to high-yield bonds.

The CDX ETF has $236 million in AUM and charges a discounted net expense ratio of 0.25% until February 2026 (Figure 4).

The CDX ETF also hedges extreme credit downside by holding a basket of high-quality stocks and shorting a basket of low-quality ("junk") stocks (Figure 5).

In Simplify's opinion, this quality/junk ("Q-J") hedge is superior to traditional credit hedges like credit default swaps ("CDS") or put options on high-yield ETFs like the iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (HYG) because it does not require the CDX ETF to pay a 'carry'.

Most forms of hedges, such as CDS or put options, require the hedger to pay periodic 'carry', akin to insurance premiums. Thus, hedges tend to cost money overall, and are called 'negative carry'. However, CDX's Q-J hedge requires no periodic payments and has historically delivered positive total returns in backtests, so it is considered a 'positive carry' hedge (Figure 6).

Hedges Can Get 'Yippy' At Extremes

On paper, the CDX ETF looks like a fantastic product, since investors can gain exposure to high-yield bonds with a downside hedge against extreme market events. However, borrowing a term from President Trump, markets can get 'yippy' at extremes, and what looks good on paper may not work in reality.

The volatile price action we saw in early April, down 7% from April 2nd to April 4th, up 14% from April 4th to April 9th, and down 9% on April 10th, is likely the result of 'basis risk' (Figure 7).

As I wrote in my prior article:

Furthermore, another risk to CDX is the basis between credit and equity risk. Effectively, CDX is trying to use the heightened equity downside risks on low-quality stocks to hedge the credit risk in its high-yield bond portfolio. While the theory is good, in practice, there may be periods when this hedge will not work.

This is essentially what happened in early April.

The good news is that the CDX ETF appears to have emerged relatively unscathed from Trump's 'Liberation Day' and has vastly outperformed other credit funds like the HYG ETF or the Janus Henderson B-BBB CLO ETF (JBBB). CDX has proved the naysayers wrong.

However, the bad news is that if investors experienced the plunge on April 4th, they could have panicked and sold their shares, missing the subsequent rebound. Investors considering the CDX ETF must have trust in the portfolio management team at Simplify to adhere to their mandate and 'ride through' any short-term volatility.

Trust Takes Years To Earn, But Can Be Lost Overnight

Unfortunately, as I have alluded to at the beginning of this article, I have recently lost some faith in Simplify's portfolio managers after repeatedly seeing them deviate from their assigned mandates. For example, the HIGH ETF was supposed to sell spreads on equity and credit indices, but was found to have sold directional puts on highly volatile single stocks like Strategy (MSTR), which directly led to a steep drawdown after Trump's 'Liberation Day' (Figure 8).

Similarly, a look at CDX's portfolio shows signs of mandate creep (Figure 9).

While the core positions in the Q-J hedge and HYG total return swaps are still present, we also see a curious new position, "OTC USDcHKDp 7.80 7/8/25 JPM". Although my OTC security naming skills are a little rusty, this looks like an OTC option bet on the USD/HKD exchange rate, struck with JPMorgan.

Putting aside the investment rationale behind this bet, perhaps the manager has a view that the Hong Kong Dollar will de-peg from the U.S. dollar over the next year, we should ask the basic question: Is this part of the mandate?

To my understanding, the CDX ETF's mandate is to deliver the total returns of high-yield bonds, hedged against adverse credit events with a long-short portfolio of high-quality stocks against low-quality stocks. The CDX ETF can also opportunistically use traditional credit hedges like CDS and put options. Nowhere in this understanding is the mention of speculation in foreign exchange derivatives.

Once again, we see the Simplify portfolio managers deviating from their mandates. In my opinion, if Mike Green, the PM for the CDX ETF, feels strongly about the USD/HKD bet, he should place it in an unconstrained macro fund that is designed to bet in any sector/strategy; something like his defunct Simplify Macro Strategy ETF, for example. He should not muddy the management of the CDX ETF with trades outside of its mandate.

Conclusion

Revisiting the CDX ETF after 6 months, I am happy to report that the CDX ETF has delivered strong returns, solidly outperforming other credit strategies and proving naysayers wrong.

However, similar to other Simplify ETFs like the HIGH and SVOL ETFs, we see mandate creep with the CDX ETF holding positions that do not align with its mandate.

I continue to believe CDX's strategy of using a long-quality/short-junk equity portfolio to hedge credit risk is a brilliant idea. However, due to heightened operational risk with Simplify's portfolio managers, I am downgrading the CDX ETF to a hold recommendation.

data.title: Apple Stock Price Struggles To Rebound As Price Slips Toward $194 Support
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

Apple Inc. (NASDAQ:AAPL, TSX:AAPL:CA) shares continued to face headwinds on Wednesday, closing at $196.08 as the stock struggled to reclaim the psychologically significant $200 threshold. The price has remained locked in a broader declining structure since March, with lower highs compressing against a weakening base around the $195 zone.

Bearish technicals dominate despite brief support hold

The technical landscape shows Apple’s recent failure to sustain upward momentum. The price remains below its 20-day and 50-day exponential moving averages, both of which are trending lower. Repeated rejections near the $197.50–$198.00 range have capped rebound efforts, while the 100- and 200-day EMAs at $204 and $211 remain distant upside targets.

Bollinger Bands are narrowing sharply, and the price is hugging the lower band, a sign of low volatility and limited buying interest. Momentum indicators remain weak. The daily RSI is flat near 47, and the MACD histogram continues to show subdued momentum, while Parabolic SAR dots remain above price on lower timeframes. The directional movement index suggests a weak trend, with sellers still slightly in control.

Support at risk as selling pressure persists

The $194–$195 zone has emerged as short-term support, but repeated tests are eroding its strength. A close below $194 could trigger a deeper decline toward the $190.28 Fibonacci pivot, followed by $186 or even $180, where historical demand may reappear. On the upside, bulls would need to reclaim $198.50 and close above $200 to stabilize the trend. A further break above $204.50 would be needed to re-engage any sustained bullish momentum.

In earlier reports, we highlighted the ongoing bearish alignment in Apple’s chart and flagged the $194–$195 zone as a critical defense line. The current structure confirms that unless this area holds, risk remains tilted to the downside into late June.

This material may contain third-party opinions, none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.
